title: Introduction to Pharmacology
summary: الفارماكولوجي هو علم دراسة الأدوية وتفاعلها مع الخلايا الحية، ويشمل الحركية الدوائية (Pharmacokinetics) أي ما يفعله الجسم بالدواء عبر ADME، والديناميكا الدوائية (Pharmacodynamics) أي ما يفعله الدواء بالجسم. تُصنَّف الأدوية إما علاجياً حسب تأثيرها السريري، أو فارماكولوجياً حسب آلية عملها الجزيئية. تمر الأدوية بأربع مراحل سريرية (Phase I-IV) قبل وصولها للمريض، وتستغرق 12-15 سنة وتكلف مليارات الدولارات. الاستخدام الرشيد للأدوية مسؤولية مشتركة لجميع أعضاء الفريق الصحي لتحقيق أقصى فائدة بأقل ضرر.

key_drugs.name: Aspirin
key_drugs.note: مثال على الاستخدام العرضي (Symptomatic) لتخفيف الألم - له اسم كيميائي Acetylsalicylic acid واسم تجاري Jusparin

key_drugs.name: Penicillin
key_drugs.note: مثال على الأدوية الطبيعية المشتقة من الكائنات الدقيقة (Micro-organisms)

key_drugs.name: Human Insulin
key_drugs.note: مثال على التخليق البيولوجي عبر Recombinant DNA technology

mnemonics.title: ADME - رحلة الدواء في الجسم
mnemonics.body:
ADME = Absorption, Distribution, Metabolism, Excretion
تذكر: الدواء يُمتص ويُوزع ويُحوَّل ويُطرح
الجملة: 'أدخل دماغك المعلومة ثم اطرحها في الامتحان'

mnemonics.title: PK و PD - الجسم والدواء
mnemonics.body:
PharmacoKinetics = الجسم يتحرك مع الدواء - تخيل الجسم (K للـ Kinetic = حركة) يمسك الدواء ويأخذه في رحلة ADME
PharmacoDynamics = الدواء يتحرك مع الجسم - الدواء (D للـ Dynamic) يضرب مستقبلات الجسم ويغير وظائفه

mnemonics.title: مراحل التجارب السريرية - SECP
mnemonics.body:
Phase I = Safety (سلامة) - 20-100 متطوع سليم
Phase II = Efficacy (فاعلية) - مئات المرضى
Phase III = Confirmatory (تأكيد) - آلاف المرضى
Phase IV = Post-marketing (بعد الموافقة) - مراقبة الآثار النادرة
تذكر: 'سأكمل التجربة' = Safety, Efficacy, Confirmatory, Post-marketing

mnemonics.title: تصنيفات الحمل A-B-C-D-X
mnemonics.body: تخيل سلماً من 5 درجات: الدرجة A في القاع (آمن تماماً كالأرض الصلبة) تصعد للأعلى حتى X في القمة (محظور كالسقوط من الجبل). كلما صعدت الدرجة زاد الخطر على الجنين، وX = eXtreme danger = منع مطلق

mnemonics.title: الآثار الجانبية - ABCDI
mnemonics.body:
A = Augmented (تضخيم الأثر الدوائي - dose-dependent)
B = Bizarre (غريب - hypersensitivity, idiosyncratic - غير مرتبط بالجرعة)
C = Chronic (مزمن - من الاستخدام الطويل أو الإيقاف المفاجئ مثل Corticosteroids)
D = Delayed (متأخر - Teratogenicity, Carcinogenicity)
I = Iatrogenic (سببه الطبيب - مرض أحدثه الدواء)

flashcards.front: What is the definition of Pharmacology?
flashcards.back: The study of drugs and how they interact with living cells

flashcards.front: What is the difference between Pharmacokinetics and Pharmacodynamics?
flashcards.back:
PK = what the body does to the drug (ADME)
PD = what the drug does to the body (effects and mechanisms of action)

flashcards.front: What are the steps of ADME?
flashcards.back: Absorption - Distribution - Metabolism - Excretion

flashcards.front: What are the main uses of drugs?
flashcards.back:
1. Symptomatic (relief of symptoms)
2. Preventative (prophylactic)
3. Diagnostic
4. Curative (therapeutic)
5. Health Maintenance

flashcards.front: What is the difference between the therapeutic and pharmacologic classification of drugs?
flashcards.back:
Therapeutic: based on the clinical effect (e.g., Anticoagulants)
Pharmacologic: more specific, based on the molecular mechanism of action (e.g., ACE inhibitors)

flashcards.front: How many phases are there in clinical trials and how many participants are in each phase?
flashcards.back:
Phase I: 20-100 healthy volunteers (Safety)
Phase II: hundreds of patients (Efficacy)
Phase III: hundreds to thousands of patients (Confirmatory)
Phase IV: after approval (Post-marketing)

flashcards.front: What are the three main sources of drugs?
flashcards.back:
1. Natural: organic (plants, animals, micro-organisms) or inorganic (minerals)
2. Synthetic: chemical or biological (Recombinant DNA)
3. Semisynthetic: such as Morphine derivatives

flashcards.front: What are the types of drug names? Give an example of each.
flashcards.back:
1. Chemical name: Acetylsalicylic acid
2. Generic (nonproprietary) name: Aspirin
3. Trade/Brand name: Jusparin, Aspocid

flashcards.front: Why is the generic name preferred when prescribing?
flashcards.back: It gives the patient more options and reduces cost

flashcards.front: What is the definition of Bioavailability?
flashcards.back: The physiological ability of a drug to reach its target cells and produce its effect

flashcards.front: When are two drugs considered bioequivalent?
flashcards.back: When they show comparable bioavailability and a similar time to reach peak blood concentration

flashcards.front: What is Category X classification in pregnancy?
flashcards.back: Absolute fetal abnormalities - absolute contraindication for use (Absolute CONTRAINDICATION)

flashcards.front: What is Category A classification in pregnancy?
flashcards.back: No risk to the fetus has been demonstrated in any trimester

flashcards.front: What is a Bizarre adverse effect? Give an example.
flashcards.back: An adverse effect that is not dose-dependent - such as hypersensitivity reactions and idiosyncratic reactions

flashcards.front: How many years does it take to develop a new drug and how much does it cost?
flashcards.back: 12-15 years and costs billions of dollars

flashcards.front: What is the main therapeutic goal of a drug?
flashcards.back: To achieve drug concentrations at the target site that produce the desired effect with minimal or no adverse effects

flashcards.front: What is Pharmacogenetics?
flashcards.back: The study of how the genome alters the body's response to drugs

flashcards.front: What is Controlled Substances Category I?
flashcards.back: Drugs with high abuse potential and no accepted medical use

flashcards.front: Define Pharmacology
flashcards.back: The science of studying drugs and how they interact with living cells.

flashcards.front: Define Drug
flashcards.back: Any chemical substance that produces a change in a biological function through its chemical action.

flashcards.front: What is Pharmacotherapeutics?
flashcards.back: The study of the clinical use of drugs to treat diseases.

flashcards.front: What is Pharmacogenetics?
flashcards.back: The study of how genes alter an individual's response to drugs.

flashcards.front: What is Pharmacoeconomics?
flashcards.back: The study of the cost-effectiveness of drug therapy.

flashcards.front: What is Pharmacoepidemiology?
flashcards.back: The study of the effects of drugs at the level of communities/populations.

flashcards.front: List the sources of drugs
flashcards.back: Plant, animal, mineral, synthetic, and biotechnology sources.

options: What the drug does to the body
What the body does to the drug
mcqs.explanation: PK = what the body does to the drug, and it includes ADME. PD is what the drug does to the body.

mcqs.q: Which phase of clinical trials involves 20-100 healthy volunteers and primarily assesses safety?
mcqs.explanation: Phase I involves 20-100 healthy volunteers and assesses safety, tolerability, and basic pharmacokinetics.

mcqs.q: A drug classified as Category X in pregnancy means:
options: No risk demonstrated in any trimester
Absolute contraindication due to fetal abnormalities
mcqs.explanation: Category X = absolute fetal abnormalities and an absolute contraindication for use during pregnancy.

mcqs.q: Human insulin produced by recombinant DNA technology is an example of which drug source?
mcqs.explanation: Biological synthesis using Recombinant DNA technology is classified as Synthetic biological.

mcqs.q: Post-marketing surveillance (Phase IV) is mainly conducted to:
options: Compare with placebo in large populations
Detect rare or long-term adverse effects
mcqs.explanation: Phase IV is conducted after official approval to detect rare or long-term adverse effects and to assess real-world effectiveness.

mcqs.q: Which adverse effect type is dose-dependent and represents an extension of the pharmacological effect?
mcqs.explanation: Augmented = an exaggeration of the normal pharmacological effect and is dose-dependent.

mcqs.q: Two drugs are considered bioequivalent when they have:
options: Comparable bioavailability and similar peak blood concentration times
mcqs.explanation: Bioequivalence means comparable bioavailability and similar times to reach peak blood concentration, with the same active ingredient and dose.
Pharmacogenetics = the study of how genetics affects the body's response to drugs.

mcqs.q: Which of the following is a diagnostic use of drugs?
mcqs.explanation: Diagnostic use aims to determine the presence of disease, such as radioactive dyes. Aspirin = Symptomatic, antibiotics = Curative, vaccine = Preventative.

mcqs.q: Teratogenicity and carcinogenicity are classified as which type of adverse drug reaction?
mcqs.explanation: Teratogenicity and carcinogenicity are both delayed effects = Delayed adverse effects.

mcqs.q: Using Aspirin to relieve pain is considered which type of use?
mcqs.explanation: Relieving the symptom without removing the cause = Symptomatic.

mcqs.q: 'What the body does to the drug' describes:
mcqs.explanation: PK = ADME = what the body does to the drug.

mcqs.q: The Hepatitis B vaccine is an example of which use?
